• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Egg neglect behaviour in biparental seabird, the Little Auk Alle alle

Martyan Gajzmer

2026-04-11

1 Diurnal pattern of exits (neglect, frequency) in Little Auk

In this section, we analyze the pattern of exiting the nest during incubation to determine the general rhytm and if there are sex-specific differences in the diurnal rhythm of nest exits.

Model and results

To model the diurnal pattern of exits, we used a Bayesian Poisson GLM with B-splines. The model structure is defined as follows:

\[\begin{aligned} G_{i} &\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \\ \log(\lambda_i) &= \alpha_{sex[i]} + \sum_{k=1}^{6} w_{k, sex[i]} B_{k,i} \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 1) \\ w_{k,j} &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 0.4) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(G_i\) is the number of gaps (exits) per hour, \(\alpha\) is the sex-specific intercept, and \(w_k\) are the weights for the spline basis \(B_k\).

library(rethinking)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(splines)
library(dplyr)

# Global Visual Settings
stage_names  <- c("early", "mid", "late")
stage_colors <- c("#3B6D11", "#185FA5", "#993556")
sex_labels   <- c("Female", "Male")
sex_col      <- c("darkorange", "navyblue")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. NEST-LEVEL DATA PREPARATION
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
E <- readRDS("./EDA_exit_hourly.RDS")

E <- E %>%
  mutate(
    incubation_stage = case_when(
      day_prior_hatch >= 21 & day_prior_hatch <= 30 ~ "early",
      day_prior_hatch >= 11 & day_prior_hatch <= 20 ~ "mid",
      day_prior_hatch >= 0  & day_prior_hatch <= 10 ~ "late",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    stage_id = as.integer(factor(incubation_stage, levels = stage_names)),
    pair_id  = as.integer(factor(Pair_status, levels = c("New", "Old"))),
    stage_pair_id = as.integer(factor(
      paste(incubation_stage, Pair_status, sep = "_"),
      levels = c("early_New", "early_Old", "mid_New", "mid_Old", "late_New", "late_Old")
    ))
  )

# Define Spline Basis (Hour sequence 0-23)
hour_seq <- 0:23
basis    <- bs(hour_seq, df = 6, degree = 3, intercept = FALSE)
basis_cols <- as.data.frame(basis)
colnames(basis_cols) <- paste0("B", 1:6)
basis_list <- as.list(basis_cols)

E_basis <- bind_cols(E, basis_cols[match(E$hour, hour_seq), ])

# --- PRIOR PREDICTIVE SIMULATION (M2) ---
set.seed(10)
plot(NULL, xlim=c(0,23), ylim=c(0,5), xlab="Hour", ylab="Expected exits", xaxt="n", main="Prior check")
axis(1, at=0:23)
for(i in 1:50){
  a_sim <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.8)
  ws_sim <- rnorm(6, 0, 0.5)
  mu_sim <- exp(a_sim + as.matrix(basis_cols) %*% ws_sim)
  lines(hour_seq, mu_sim, col=col.alpha("black", 0.25))
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. SEX-SPECIFIC DATA PREPARATION
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
S <- readRDS("EDA_sex_neglect_wide.RDS")
G <- readRDS("EDA_sex_gaps_hourly.RDS")

sex_hourly_raw <- G %>%
  filter(!is.na(responsible_sex)) %>%
  group_by(season, session, nest, sx = responsible_sex, hour) %>%
  summarise(n_gaps_per_hour = n(), .groups = "drop")

meta <- S %>%
  mutate(
    season = as.character(season),
    incubation_stage = case_when(
      day_prior_hatch >= 21 & day_prior_hatch <= 30 ~ "early",
      day_prior_hatch >= 11 & day_prior_hatch <= 20 ~ "mid",
      day_prior_hatch >= 0  & day_prior_hatch <= 10 ~ "late",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    stage_id = as.integer(factor(incubation_stage, levels = stage_names)),
    sex_id   = as.integer(factor(sx, levels = c("f", "m"))),
    stage_sex_id = as.integer(factor(paste(incubation_stage, sx, sep = "_"),
                                     levels = c("early_f", "early_m", "mid_f", "mid_m", "late_f", "late_m")))
  ) %>%
  select(season, session, nest, sx, day_prior_hatch, incubation_stage, stage_id, sex_id, stage_sex_id)

E_sex <- meta %>% crossing(hour = 0:23) %>%
  left_join(sex_hourly_raw, by = c("season", "session", "nest", "sx", "hour")) %>%
  mutate(n_gaps_per_hour = replace_na(n_gaps_per_hour, 0))

E_by_sex <- E_sex %>% group_by(sex_id, hour) %>% summarise(mean_gaps = mean(n_gaps_per_hour), .groups = "drop")
E_stage_sex <- E_sex %>% group_by(stage_sex_id, hour) %>% summarise(mean_gaps = mean(n_gaps_per_hour), .groups = "drop")

# Re-scaling Splines for Sex Data
E_fit_sex <- E_sex %>% filter(!is.na(sex_id))
B_sex <- bs(E_fit_sex$hour, df = 6, degree = 3, intercept = TRUE)
B_pred_sex <- bs(hour_seq, knots = attr(B_sex, "knots"), Boundary.knots = attr(B_sex, "Boundary.knots"), degree = 3, intercept = TRUE)
B_scaled_sex <- scale(B_sex)
B_pred_scaled_sex <- scale(B_pred_sex, center = attr(B_scaled_sex, "scaled:center"), scale = attr(B_scaled_sex, "scaled:scale"))
basis_cols_sex <- setNames(as.data.frame(B_scaled_sex), paste0("B", 1:ncol(B_sex)))
basis_list_sex <- as.list(setNames(as.data.frame(B_pred_scaled_sex), paste0("B", 1:ncol(B_sex))))
post_f <- link(m1_sex, data = c(list(sex_id = rep(1, 24)), basis_list_sex))
mu_f   <- apply(post_f, 2, mean)
PI_f   <- apply(post_f, 2, PI, prob = 0.89)

# Predictions for Males (sex_id = 2)
post_m <- link(m1_sex, data = c(list(sex_id = rep(2, 24)), basis_list_sex))
mu_m   <- apply(post_m, 2, mean)
PI_m   <- apply(post_m, 2, PI, prob = 0.89)

mu_f_m1 <- link(m1_sex, data = c(list(sex_id = rep(1, 24)), basis_list_sex))
mu_m_m1 <- link(m1_sex, data = c(list(sex_id = rep(2, 24)), basis_list_sex))
avg_rate_f_m1 <- apply(mu_f_m1, 1, mean); avg_rate_m_m1 <- apply(mu_m_m1, 1, mean); diff_fm_m1 <- avg_rate_f_m1 - avg_rate_m_m1
for (s in 1:2) {
  pred_s <- link(m1_sex, data = c(list(sex_id = rep(s, 24)), basis_list_sex))
  peak_h <- apply(pred_s, 1, which.max) - 1
  cat(sex_labels[s], "Peak Hour:", round(mean(peak_h), 1), "\n")
}
## Female Peak Hour: 4.8 
## Male Peak Hour: 5.7
avg_rate_f_m1 <- apply(post_f, 1, mean)
avg_rate_m_m1 <- apply(post_m, 1, mean)
diff_fm_m1    <- avg_rate_f_m1 - avg_rate_m_m1

cat("Female mean rate:", round(mean(avg_rate_f_m1), 3),
    " 89% PI [", round(PI(avg_rate_f_m1, prob=0.89), 3), "]\n")
## Female mean rate: 0.087  89% PI [ 0.081 0.093 ]
cat("Male mean rate:  ", round(mean(avg_rate_m_m1), 3),
    " 89% PI [", round(PI(avg_rate_m_m1, prob=0.89), 3), "]\n")
## Male mean rate:   0.121  89% PI [ 0.113 0.128 ]
cat("Difference (F-M):", round(mean(diff_fm_m1), 3),
    " 89% PI [", round(PI(diff_fm_m1, prob=0.89), 3), "]\n")
## Difference (F-M): -0.034  89% PI [ -0.043 -0.025 ]
cat("P(Female < Male):", round(mean(diff_fm_m1 < 0), 3), "\n")
## P(Female < Male): 1

Males initiated gaps at a notably higher rate than females across the day (male mean: 0.121 gaps/hour, 89% PI [0.113, 0.128]; female mean: 0.087 gaps/hour, 89% PI [0.081, 0.093]). The difference was consistent and credible, with females showing reliably fewer exits than males (difference: −0.034, 89% PI [−0.043, −0.025]; P(Female < Male) = 1.0).

Both sexes showed a similar diel pattern, with gap frequency peaking in the early morning hours (female peak: ~5 AM, male peak: ~6 AM), suggesting a shared response to time-of-day cues rather than sex-specific scheduling of nest attendance.

plot_hour_df <- data.frame(
  hour   = hour_seq,
  f_mu   = apply(post_f, 2, mean), 
  f_low  = apply(post_f, 2, PI, prob = 0.89)[1,], 
  f_high = apply(post_f, 2, PI, prob = 0.89)[2,],
  m_mu   = apply(post_m, 2, mean), 
  m_low  = apply(post_m, 2, PI, prob = 0.89)[1,], 
  m_high = apply(post_m, 2, PI, prob = 0.89)[2,]
)

col_female <- "darkorange"
col_male   <- "navyblue"

ggplot(plot_hour_df, aes(x = hour)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = f_low, ymax = f_high), fill = col_female, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = m_low, ymax = m_high), fill = col_male, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(aes(y = f_mu), color = col_female, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = m_mu), color = col_male, linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.17, label = "\u2642", size = 10, color = col_male) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.07, label = "\u2640", size = 10, color = col_female) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 23, by = 2), limits = c(0, 23)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 0.25), clip = "off") +
  labs(x = "Hour of day", y = "Expected exits") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

Expected number of exits per hour by sex

2 Neglect behaviour across incubation

In this section we model three complementary aspects of egg neglect across incubation days: how often gaps occur (frequency), whether any neglect happens at all on a given day (probability), and how long neglect lasts when it does occur (duration). Together these capture both the occurrence and intensity of neglect as hatching approaches.

2.1 Gap frequency across incubation

We modelled the daily number of incubation gaps using a Poisson GLM with a linear effect of days prior to hatching, fit separately for each sex. Day was mean-centered prior to fitting.

\[\begin{aligned} N_i &\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \\ \log(\lambda_i) &= \alpha_{sex[i]} + \beta_{sex[i]} \cdot \text{dph}_c \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(1,\, 0.5) \\ \beta_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.05) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(N_i\) is the number of gaps on observation \(i\), \(\text{dph}_c\) is days prior to hatch (mean-centered), and parameters vary by sex \(j\).

set.seed(123)
n_sim <- 100
prior_mu_ov <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = length(DAY_SEQ))

for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(1, 1, 0.5)
  b <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.05)
  prior_mu_ov[i,] <- exp(a + b * dph_seq_c_ov)
}

matplot(DAY_SEQ, t(prior_mu_ov), type = "l", lty = 1, xlim = c(28, 2),
        col = col.alpha("black", 0.3),
        xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "Expected n_gaps",
        main = "Prior check: gap frequency")

Prior predictive simulation for gap frequency model 

cat("Female slope:", round(mean(post_s_samp$b[,1]), 4),
    "PI:", round(PI(post_s_samp$b[,1]), 4),
    "P(<0):", mean(post_s_samp$b[,1] < 0), "\n")
## Female slope: 0.0594 PI: 0.0498 0.069 P(<0): 0
cat("Male slope:  ", round(mean(post_s_samp$b[,2]), 4),
    "PI:", round(PI(post_s_samp$b[,2]), 4),
    "P(<0):", mean(post_s_samp$b[,2] < 0), "\n")
## Male slope:   0.0682 PI: 0.06 0.0765 P(<0): 0
cat("Mean gap diff (F-M):", round(mean(gap_diff), 2),
    "PI:", round(PI(gap_diff), 2), "\n")
## Mean gap diff (F-M): -0.66 PI: -0.88 -0.43
cat("P(Female > Male baseline):", mean(post_s_samp$a[,1] > post_s_samp$a[,2]), "\n")
## P(Female > Male baseline): 0

Both sexes showed a consistent decrease in gap frequency as incubation progressed toward hatching. Female gap frequency increased by 0.059 per day (89% PI [0.050, 0.069]; P(slope < 0) = 0), and males showed a similarly positive trend of 0.068 per day (89% PI [0.060, 0.077]; P(slope < 0) = 0), suggesting both parents become progressively more attentive as the egg approaches hatching.

Males initiated more gaps than females throughout incubation, with a baseline difference of −0.66 gaps per day (89% PI [−0.88, −0.43]; P(Female > Male) = 0), indicating that the male-biased pattern of nest leaving established in the diurnal analysis persists across the entire incubation period. The slopes of both sexes were similar, suggesting the sex difference in gap frequency is relatively stable across incubation rather than diverging or converging as hatching approaches.

col_female <- "darkorange"; col_male <- "navyblue"

col_female <- "darkorange" 
col_male   <- "navyblue" 

# 3. Create the Plot
ggplot() +
  # Ribbons
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_gaps_df, aes(x = day, ymin = f_low, ymax = f_high),  
              fill = col_female, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_gaps_df, aes(x = day, ymin = m_low, ymax = m_high),  
              fill = col_male, alpha = 0.15) +
  labs(title = "A)")+
  
  # Mean Lines
  geom_line(data = plot_gaps_df, aes(x = day, y = f_mu), color = col_female, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_line(data = plot_gaps_df, aes(x = day, y = m_mu), color = col_male, linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  # Bubble Points (Raw n_gaps data)
  geom_count(data = E_fit_sex, aes(x = day_prior_hatch, y = n_gaps, 
                                   color = as.factor(sex_id)), alpha = 0.4) +
  
  # Manual Color Scales
  scale_color_manual(values = c("1" = col_female, "2" = col_male), guide = "none") +
  
  # Sex Labels (Using same multipliers as your neglect plot for consistency)
  annotate("text", x = 27.8, y = max(plot_gaps_df$m_mu) * 1.25, 
           label = "\u2642", size = 9, color = col_male, fontface = "bold", family = "sans") +
  annotate("text", x = 27.8, y = min(plot_gaps_df$f_mu) * 3, 
           label = "\u2640", size = 9, color = col_female, fontface = "bold", family = "sans") +
  
  # Scaling (Linear scale for n_gaps, reversed X for time)
  scale_x_reverse(breaks = seq(2, 28, by = 5)) +
  ylim(0, 15) +
  scale_size_continuous(name = "n obs", range = c(1, 7)) +
  labs(x = "Days prior hatch", y = "Number of neglect gaps") +
  
  # --- THE "PRETTY" ADJUSTMENTS (Identical to your neglect plot) ---
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") + 
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = c(0.90, 0.85),
    legend.background = element_blank(),
    
    # Matching fonts and sizes exactly
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 13, face = "plain", family = "sans", color = "black"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 13, face = "plain", family = "sans", color = "black"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 11, family = "sans", color = "black"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11, family = "sans", color = "black"),
    
    legend.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "plain", family = "sans"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10, family = "sans"),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

Expected number of neglect gaps per day by sex across incubation

2.2 Probability of any neglect

Not every observation day results in neglect. We therefore modelled the probability that any neglect occurred on a given day using a binomial GLM with a logit link, allowing intercepts and slopes to vary by sex.

\[\begin{aligned} Y_i &\sim \text{Binomial}(1,\, p_i) \\ \text{logit}(p_i) &= \alpha_{sex[i]} + \beta_{sex[i]} \cdot \text{dph}_c \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 1) \\ \beta_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.2) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(Y_i\) is a binary indicator of whether any neglect was recorded on observation \(i\), and \(p_i\) is the probability of neglect. The prior on \(\alpha\) is weakly informative on the logit scale, placing most prior weight on probabilities between 0.1 and 0.9.

set.seed(42)
n_sim    <- 100
dph_grid <- seq(min(D_occ$dph_c), max(D_occ$dph_c), length.out = 100)

prior_f_occ <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = length(dph_grid))
prior_m_occ <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = length(dph_grid))

for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(2, 0, 1)
  b <- rnorm(2, 0, 0.2)
  prior_f_occ[i,] <- inv_logit(a[1] + b[1] * dph_grid)
  prior_m_occ[i,] <- inv_logit(a[2] + b[2] * dph_grid)
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
matplot(dph_grid, t(prior_f_occ), type = "l", lty = 1,
        col = col.alpha("darkorange", 0.3),
        ylim = c(0, 1),
        xlab = "dph (centered)", ylab = "P(neglect)",
        main = "Prior: Female")
matplot(dph_grid, t(prior_m_occ), type = "l", lty = 1,
        col = col.alpha("navyblue", 0.3),
        ylim = c(0, 1),
        xlab = "dph (centered)", ylab = "P(neglect)",
        main = "Prior: Male")

Prior predictive simulation for probability of neglect model 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
cat("Female slope:", round(mean(post_occ_samp$b[,1]), 4),
    " PI:", round(PI(post_occ_samp$b[,1]), 4),
    " P(<0):", round(mean(post_occ_samp$b[,1] < 0), 3), "\n")
## Female slope: 0.0548  PI: 0.026 0.084  P(<0): 0.001
cat("Male slope:  ", round(mean(post_occ_samp$b[,2]), 4),
    " PI:", round(PI(post_occ_samp$b[,2]), 4),
    " P(<0):", round(mean(post_occ_samp$b[,2] < 0), 3), "\n")
## Male slope:   0.0691  PI: 0.0336 0.1041  P(<0): 0.001
cat("Mean prob diff (F-M):", round(mean(prob_diff), 3),
    " PI:", round(PI(prob_diff), 3), "\n")
## Mean prob diff (F-M): -0.183  PI: -0.252 -0.114
cat("P(Female > Male baseline):", round(mean(post_occ_samp$a[,1] > post_occ_samp$a[,2]), 3), "\n")
## P(Female > Male baseline): 0

The probability of neglect occurring on any given day decreased as hatching approached for both sexes, with both slopes positive on the reversed time axis (female: 0.055, 89% PI [0.026, 0.084], P(slope < 0) = 0.001; male: 0.069, 89% PI [0.034, 0.105], P(slope < 0) = 0.001). This indicates that neglect was more common earlier in incubation and became less likely as the egg approached hatching, consistent with increasing parental attentiveness near the end of incubation.

Males were consistently more likely to neglect than females throughout incubation, with a baseline probability difference of −0.183 (89% PI [−0.252, −0.115]; P(Female > Male) = 0), confirming that the male-biased pattern seen in gap frequency also holds when simply asking whether any neglect occurred at all on a given day.

plot_occ <- data.frame(
  day = DAY_SEQ,
  f_mu = mu_f_occ, f_low = PI_f_occ[1,], f_high = PI_f_occ[2,],
  m_mu = mu_m_occ, m_low = PI_m_occ[1,], m_high = PI_m_occ[2,]
)

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_occ,
              aes(x = day, ymin = f_low, ymax = f_high),
              fill = col_female, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_occ,
              aes(x = day, ymin = m_low, ymax = m_high),
              fill = col_male, alpha = 0.15) +
  labs(title = "C)") +
  
  geom_line(data = plot_occ,
            aes(x = day, y = f_mu),
            color = col_female, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_line(data = plot_occ,
            aes(x = day, y = m_mu),
            color = col_male, linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  geom_count(data = D_occ,
             aes(x = day_prior_hatch,
                 y = any_neglect,
                 color = as.factor(sex_id)),
             alpha = 0.4) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = c("1" = col_female, "2" = col_male),
                     guide = "none") +
  
  annotate("text", x = 28.8, y = 0.88,
           label = "\u2642", size = 9,
           color = col_male, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 28.8, y = 0.75,
           label = "\u2640", size = 9,
           color = col_female, fontface = "bold") +
  
  scale_x_reverse(breaks = seq(2, 28, by = 5)) +
  ylim(0, 1) +
  scale_size_continuous(name = "n obs", range = c(1, 6)) +
  labs(x = "Days prior hatch", y = "Probability of neglect") +
  
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = c(0.90, 0.81),
    legend.background = element_blank(),
    legend.key = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11, color = "black"),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 11),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

Probability of any neglect per day by sex across incubation

2.3 Duration of neglect (given neglect occurred)

To capture how long neglect episodes last when they do occur, we modelled total neglect duration on the log scale using a Gaussian GLM, restricted to days where neglect was recorded. This forms the second component of the hurdle model — separating the question of whether neglect happens from how severe it is when it does.

\[\begin{aligned} \log(D_i) &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma) \\ \mu_i &= \alpha_{sex[i]} + \beta_{sex[i]} \cdot \text{dph}_c \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(6,\, 0.5) \\ \beta_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.1) \\ \sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(1) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(D_i\) is total neglect duration in seconds on observation \(i\). The prior on \(\alpha\) is centered at 6 on the log scale, corresponding to a baseline duration of roughly 400 seconds (~7 minutes), which is biologically plausible for a seabird of this size.

set.seed(42)
dph_grid_dur <- seq(min(D_dur$dph_c), max(D_dur$dph_c), length.out = 100)

prior_f_dur <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = length(dph_grid_dur))
prior_m_dur <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = length(dph_grid_dur))

for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(2, 6, 0.2)
  b <- rnorm(2, 0, 0.05)
  prior_f_dur[i,] <- a[1] + b[1] * dph_grid_dur  # no exp() here
  prior_m_dur[i,] <- a[2] + b[2] * dph_grid_dur  # no exp() here
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
matplot(dph_grid_dur, t(prior_f_dur), type = "l", lty = 1,
        col = col.alpha("darkorange", 0.3),
        ylim = c(4, 8),          # log scale limits (exp(12) ~ 160,000 sec)
        xlab = "dph (centered)", ylab = "log Duration (sec)",
        main = "Prior: Female")
matplot(dph_grid_dur, t(prior_m_dur), type = "l", lty = 1,
        col = col.alpha("navyblue", 0.3),
        ylim = c(4, 8),
        xlab = "dph (centered)", ylab = "log Duration (sec)",
        main = "Prior: Male")

Prior predictive simulation for neglect duration model 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
cat("Female slope:", round(mean(post_dur_samp$b[,1]), 4),
    " PI:", round(PI(post_dur_samp$b[,1]), 4),
    " P(<0):", round(mean(post_dur_samp$b[,1] < 0), 3), "\n")
## Female slope: 0.0633  PI: 0.0344 0.0934  P(<0): 0
cat("Male slope:  ", round(mean(post_dur_samp$b[,2]), 4),
    " PI:", round(PI(post_dur_samp$b[,2]), 4),
    " P(<0):", round(mean(post_dur_samp$b[,2] < 0), 3), "\n")
## Male slope:   0.0638  PI: 0.0368 0.0915  P(<0): 0
cat("Mean duration diff (F-M, sec):", round(mean(dur_diff), 1),
    " PI:", round(PI(dur_diff), 1), "\n")
## Mean duration diff (F-M, sec): -38  PI: -140.1 62.7
cat("P(Female > Male baseline):", round(mean(post_dur_samp$a[,1] > post_dur_samp$a[,2]), 3), "\n")
## P(Female > Male baseline): 0.275

When neglect did occur, its duration also decreased toward hatching at a near-identical rate in both sexes (female slope: 0.0633, 89% PI [0.0344, 0.0934], P(slope < 0) = 0; male slope: 0.0638, 89% PI [0.0368, 0.0915], P(slope < 0) = 0), suggesting that not only does neglect become less frequent near hatching, but individual neglect episodes also become shorter.

Unlike gap frequency and probability of neglect, the duration of neglect when it occurred did not differ credibly between sexes. The mean baseline difference was -38 seconds (89% PI [-140.1, 62.7]; P(Female > Male) = 0.275), with the interval spanning zero and the posterior showing substantial overlap. This suggests that while males neglect more often, when either sex does neglect, the duration of those episodes is broadly similar.

Taken together, the hurdle model reveals an important asymmetry: the sex difference in neglect is primarily driven by how often males initiate gaps and whether they neglect at all, rather than by differences in how long neglect lasts once it begins. Both components decline toward hatching, pointing to a shared increase in parental commitment as the critical hatching window approaches.

plot_df <- data.frame(
  day = DAY_SEQ,
  f_mu = mu_f_dur, f_low = PI_f_dur[1,], f_high = PI_f_dur[2,],
  m_mu = mu_m_dur, m_low = PI_m_dur[1,], m_high = PI_m_dur[2,]
)

ggplot() +
  # 1. Uncertainty Ribbons
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_df,
              aes(x = day, ymin = f_low, ymax = f_high),
              fill = col_female, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_df,
              aes(x = day, ymin = m_low, ymax = m_high),
              fill = col_male, alpha = 0.15) +
  
  # 2. Regression Lines
  geom_line(data = plot_df,
            aes(x = day, y = f_mu),
            color = col_female, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_line(data = plot_df,
            aes(x = day, y = m_mu),
            color = col_male, linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  # 3. Observation Bubbles 
  # Using round() ensures that close values overlap to create "n obs" > 1
  geom_point(data = D_dur,
             aes(x = day_prior_hatch,
                 y = sum_neglect_sec,
                 color = as.factor(sex_id)),
             alpha = 0.4, 
             size = 2) + #
  
  # 4. Sex Annotations (Placed at the start of the lines)
  annotate("text", x = 27.8, y = plot_df$m_mu[100] * 3, 
           label = "\u2642", size = 10, color = col_male, fontface = "bold", family = "sans") +
  annotate("text", x = 27.8, y = plot_df$f_mu[100] * 0.3, 
           label = "\u2640", size = 10, color = col_female, fontface = "bold", family = "sans") +
  
  # 5. Scales and Labels
  scale_color_manual(values = c("1" = col_female, "2" = col_male), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_number(),
                breaks = scales::trans_breaks("log10", function(x) 10^x)) +
  scale_x_reverse(breaks = seq(2, 28, by = 5)) +
  
  # 6. Size Legend (Fixed to whole numbers 1 and 2 only)
  scale_size_continuous(name = "n obs", 
                        range = c(1.5, 4), 
                        breaks = c(1, 2),
                        labels = c("1", "2")) +
  
  labs(title = "B)", 
       x = "Days prior hatch",
       y = "Duration of neglect (sec, log scale)") +
  
  # 7. Theme and Font Adjustments
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = c(0.90, 0.85),      # Nudged down slightly to avoid border
    legend.background = element_blank(), 
    legend.key = element_blank(),        # Transparent legend keys
    
    # Matching all text to Arial/Sans
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 13, family = "sans"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 13, family = "sans"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 11, color = "black", family = "sans"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11, color = "black", family = "sans"),
    
    legend.title = element_text(size = 11, family = "sans"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10, family = "sans"),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

Total neglect duration (seconds, log scale) given neglect occurred, by sex across incubation 

# View plot

3 Neglect behaviour in relation to pair status and similarity

In this section we examine whether pair experience (new vs. established pairs) and pair similarity predict neglect behaviour. We model both gap frequency and total neglect duration for each predictor separately, using a hurdle approach — the frequency model includes all observations, while the duration model is restricted to days where neglect was recorded.

3.1 Pair status

We modelled gap frequency using a Poisson GLM and neglect duration using a Normal GLM on the log scale, both with pair status (New vs. Old) as a binary predictor and session as an index variable to account for repeated measures across incubation. Due to low sample size, all three sessions were included in this analysis.

\[\begin{aligned} N_i &\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \\ \log(\lambda_i) &= \alpha_{session[i]} + \beta \cdot \text{pair}_{01} \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.8) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.5) \end{aligned}\]

\[\begin{aligned} \log(D_i) &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma) \\ \mu_i &= \alpha_{session[i]} + \beta \cdot \text{pair}_{01} \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(6,\, 0.5) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.5) \\ \sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(1) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(\text{pair}_{01}\) is a binary indicator (0 = New, 1 = Old) and session \(j\) captures variation across incubation stages.

set.seed(42)
n_sim <- 100

prior_freq_new <- prior_freq_old <- numeric(n_sim)
prior_dur_new  <- prior_dur_old  <- numeric(n_sim)

for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a   <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.8)
  b   <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
  prior_freq_new[i] <- exp(a)
  prior_freq_old[i] <- exp(a + b)

  a_d <- rnorm(1, 6, 0.5)
  b_d <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
  prior_dur_new[i] <- a_d
  prior_dur_old[i] <- a_d + b_d
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
boxplot(list(New = prior_freq_new, Old = prior_freq_old),
        col = c("gray80", "gray60"),
        ylab = "Expected n gaps", main = "Prior: Frequency",
        outline = FALSE)
boxplot(list(New = prior_dur_new, Old = prior_dur_old),
        col = c("gray80", "gray60"),
        ylab = "log Duration (sec)", main = "Prior: Duration (log scale)",
        outline = FALSE)

Prior predictive simulation for pair status models 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
cat("FREQ: Rate ratio (Old/New):", round(mean(exp(post_f$b)), 3),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(exp(post_f$b)), 3), "\n")
## FREQ: Rate ratio (Old/New): 1.25  89% PI: 1.01 1.525
cat("FREQ: P(Old > New):", round(mean(post_f$b > 0), 3), "\n")
## FREQ: P(Old > New): 0.953
dur_new  <- exp(post_d$a[,1])
dur_old  <- exp(post_d$a[,1] + post_d$b)
dur_diff <- dur_old - dur_new
cat("DUR: Mean diff (Old-New, sec):", round(mean(dur_diff), 1),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(dur_diff), 1), "\n")
## DUR: Mean diff (Old-New, sec): 125.5  89% PI: -273.5 493
cat("DUR: P(Old > New):", round(mean(post_d$b > 0), 3), "\n")
## DUR: P(Old > New): 0.719

Established pairs (Old) initiated gaps more frequently than new pairs, with a rate ratio of 1.25 (89% PI [1.01, 1.53]; P(Old > New) = 0.953), suggesting that experienced pairs leave the nest more often. However, when neglect did occur, the duration of neglect episodes did not differ credibly between pair types — the mean difference was 125.5 seconds in favour of Old pairs (89% PI [−273.5, 493.0]; P(Old > New) = 0.719), with the interval spanning zero and substantial posterior uncertainty. This suggests that while established pairs neglect more frequently, the length of individual neglect episodes is similar regardless of pair experience.

p_freq <- ggplot() +
  geom_violin(data = D_all, aes(x = Pair_status, y = n_gaps),
              fill = "gray90", color = "gray80", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(data = D_all, aes(x = Pair_status, y = n_gaps),
              width = 0.2, alpha = 0.3, color = "gray40", size = 1) +
  geom_errorbar(data = plot_freq_df, aes(x = status, ymin = low, ymax = high),
                width = 0.1, linewidth = 1.2, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = plot_freq_df, aes(x = status, y = mu),
             size = 4, color = "black") +
  labs(title = "A)", x = "Pair status", y = "Number of exits") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

p_dur <- ggplot() +
  geom_violin(data = D_nonzero, aes(x = Pair_status, y = sum_gaps),
              fill = "gray90", color = "gray80", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(data = D_nonzero, aes(x = Pair_status, y = sum_gaps),
              width = 0.2, alpha = 0.3, color = "gray40", size = 1) +
  geom_errorbar(data = plot_dur_df, aes(x = status, ymin = low, ymax = high),
                width = 0.1, linewidth = 1.2, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = plot_dur_df, aes(x = status, y = mu),
             size = 4, color = "black") +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_number()) +
  labs(title = "B)", x = "Pair status", y = "Duration of neglect (sec, log scale)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

library(patchwork)
p_freq + p_dur

Gap frequency (A) and neglect duration (B) by pair status. Points show model estimates with 89% PI; violins and jittered points show raw data.

3.2 Pair similarity

We modelled gap frequency and neglect duration as a function of a continuous pair Similarity Index, using only the first incubation session as it showed the highest number of exits. No session index was needed here as only one session was included.

\[\begin{aligned} N_i &\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \\ \log(\lambda_i) &= \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{Similarity}_i \\ \alpha &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.8) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.5) \end{aligned}\]

\[\begin{aligned} \log(D_i) &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma) \\ \mu_i &= \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{Similarity}_i \\ \alpha &\sim \text{Normal}(6,\, 0.5) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.5) \\ \sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(1) \end{aligned}\]

set.seed(42)
xseq_prior <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 100)

prior_sim_freq <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = 100)
prior_sim_dur  <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sim, ncol = 100)

for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a   <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.8); b   <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
  prior_sim_freq[i,] <- exp(a + b * xseq_prior)

  a_d <- rnorm(1, 6, 0.5); b_d <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
  prior_sim_dur[i,]  <- a_d + b_d * xseq_prior  # log scale
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
matplot(xseq_prior, t(prior_sim_freq), type = "l", lty = 1,
        col = col.alpha("black", 0.3),
        xlab = "Similarity Index", ylab = "Expected n gaps",
        main = "Prior: Frequency")
matplot(xseq_prior, t(prior_sim_dur), type = "l", lty = 1,
        col = col.alpha("black", 0.3),
        xlab = "Similarity Index", ylab = "log Duration (sec)",
        main = "Prior: Duration (log scale)")

Prior predictive simulation for pair similarity models 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
cat("FREQ: Slope b:", round(mean(post_sim_f$b), 3),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(post_sim_f$b), 3), "\n")
## FREQ: Slope b: 0.542  89% PI: 0.341 0.74
cat("FREQ: P(slope > 0):", round(mean(post_sim_f$b > 0), 3), "\n")
## FREQ: P(slope > 0): 1
cat("DUR:  Slope b:", round(mean(post_sim_d$b), 3),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(post_sim_d$b), 3), "\n")
## DUR:  Slope b: 0.36  89% PI: -0.295 1.023
cat("DUR:  P(slope > 0):", round(mean(post_sim_d$b > 0), 3), "\n")
## DUR:  P(slope > 0): 0.806

Gap frequency increased credibly with pair similarity, with a slope of 0.542 on the log scale (89% PI [0.341, 0.740]; P(slope > 0) = 1.0), indicating that more similar pairs initiated more frequent nest exits. This is a strong and consistent effect with no posterior probability supporting a negative relationship.

Neglect duration showed a weaker and less credible positive trend with similarity (slope: 0.360, 89% PI [−0.295, 1.023]; P(slope > 0) = 0.806), with the interval spanning zero. While the direction is consistent with the frequency result, the evidence is insufficient to conclude that more similar pairs also show longer neglect episodes when they do occur.

Taken together, pair similarity appears to primarily influence how often pairs neglect rather than how long those episodes last — a pattern that mirrors the sex differences observed earlier, where frequency was the more consistent axis of variation.

shared_size_scale <- scale_size_continuous(
  name   = "n obs",
  range  = c(1.5, 8),
  limits = c(1, 10),
  breaks = c(1, 2, 5, 10)
)

p_sim_freq <- ggplot() +
  geom_count(data = D_sim, aes(x = Similarity_Index, y = n_gaps),
             alpha = 0.2, color = "gray40") +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_sim_f, aes(x = x, ymin = low, ymax = high),
              fill = "black", alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_line(data = plot_sim_f, aes(x = x, y = mu),
            color = "black", linewidth = 1.2) +
  shared_size_scale +
  labs(title = "A)", x = "Similarity Index", y = "Number of exits") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_blank())

p_sim_dur <- ggplot() +
  geom_count(data = D_sim_nonzero, aes(x = Similarity_Index, y = sum_gaps),
             alpha = 0.2, color = "gray40") +
  geom_ribbon(data = plot_sim_d, aes(x = x, ymin = low, ymax = high),
              fill = "black", alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_line(data = plot_sim_d, aes(x = x, y = mu),
            color = "black", linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_number()) +
  shared_size_scale +
  labs(title = "B)", x = "Similarity Index", y = "Duration of neglect (sec, log scale)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_blank())

p_sim_freq + p_sim_dur +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "right")

Gap frequency (A) and neglect duration (B) in relation to pair Similarity Index. Lines show posterior mean with 89% PI; bubble size indicates number of overlapping observations.

4 Repeatability of neglect behaviour

To determine whether individuals are consistent in their tendency to neglect across incubation sessions, we estimated the repeatability of neglect occurrence using the rptR package. We modelled whether an individual neglected at all on a given observation (binary: 0/1) using a binomial repeatability model, with session as a fixed effect to account for the systematic change in neglect across incubation stages, and individual identity (ringno) as the grouping factor. This approach asks: after accounting for when in incubation the observation was made, is an individual consistently neglectful or not across all sessions in which it was observed? Repeatability was estimated using 1000 bootstrap iterations for confidence intervals and 1000 permutations for significance testing.

\[\begin{aligned} \text{is_neglecting}_i &\sim \text{Binomial}(1,\, p_i) \\ \text{logit}(p_i) &= \beta \cdot \text{session}_i + u_{ringno[i]} \\ u_{ringno} &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, \sigma^2_{ringno}) \end{aligned}\]

Where repeatability \(R\) is defined as the proportion of total phenotypic variance attributable to among-individual differences:

\[R = \frac{\sigma^2_{ringno}}{\sigma^2_{ringno} + \sigma^2_{residual}}\]

We estimated repeatability separately for the full dataset and for each sex, to test whether the consistency of neglect tendency differs between females and males.

Repeatability of individual neglect tendency across incubation sessions. Session included as fixed effect.
Group R (Link) 95% CI (Link) R (Original) 95% CI (Original) P (Permutation)
Overall 0.287 [0.142, 0.417] 0.348 [0.153, 0.607] 0.001
Males 0.198 [0.043, 0.374] 0.234 [0.044, 0.56] 0.001
Females 0.290 [0.091, 0.44] 0.324 [0.092, 0.59] 0.001

Neglect tendency showed significant and consistent repeatability across incubation sessions. In the overall sample, individuals were moderately repeatable in whether they neglected at all, with a link-scale repeatability of R = 0.287 (95% CI [0.142, 0.417]; P < 0.001 by permutation test), and an original-scale estimate of R = 0.348 (95% CI [0.153, 0.607]). This indicates that a meaningful proportion of variation in neglect occurrence is attributable to stable among-individual differences, rather than being purely driven by session or chance.

Repeatability was significant in both sexes, with females showing somewhat higher individual consistency than males. Females had a link-scale repeatability of R = 0.290 (95% CI [0.091, 0.440]), compared to R = 0.198 (95% CI [0.043, 0.374]) in males, with both significant at P < 0.001 by permutation test. The confidence intervals of both sexes overlap substantially, so caution is warranted in interpreting this difference as biologically meaningful.

Taken together, these results suggest that neglect tendency is a repeatable individual trait in this population — some individuals consistently neglect across incubation sessions while others consistently do not. This repeatability is present in both sexes, and its consistency with the earlier finding that males initiate gaps more frequently suggests that individual differences in neglect propensity may reflect stable behavioural strategies rather than purely opportunistic responses to environmental conditions.

5 Effect of neglect on incubation duration

To test whether the cumulative amount of neglect during incubation affects how long incubation lasts, we modelled incubation duration (days) as a function of total neglect accumulated across all three sessions. Only nests with complete data across all three incubation sessions were included, to ensure total neglect was not underestimated due to missing observations. Total neglect duration was log-transformed prior to fitting to address its right-skewed distribution, and then mean-centered.

\[\begin{aligned} \text{Duration}_i &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma) \\ \mu_i &= \alpha + \beta \cdot \log(\text{neglect})_{c,i} \\ \alpha &\sim \text{Normal}(29,\, 0.5) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.5) \\ \sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(1) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(\log(\text{neglect})_{c,i}\) is the log-transformed total neglect in seconds, mean-centered. The prior on \(\alpha\) is centered at 29 days, consistent with the known incubation period of Little Auk. The prior on \(\beta\) is centered at zero, reflecting no strong prior expectation about the direction of the effect.

set.seed(123)
n_sim <- 50

plot(NULL, xlim = range(log_neglect_seq), ylim = c(26, 34),
     xlab = "log Total neglect (centered)",
     ylab = "Incubation duration (days)",
     main = "Prior predictive: incubation duration")
for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(1, 29, 0.5)
  b <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
  lines(log_neglect_seq, a + b * log_neglect_seq,
        col = col.alpha("grey", 0.6))
}
abline(h = c(28, 32), col = "red", lty = 2)

Prior predictive simulation for incubation duration model. Red dashed lines indicate the biologically plausible range of incubation duration in Little Auk. 

print(precis(m_quap))
##             mean        sd        5.5%      94.5%
## a     29.1382123 0.1628692 28.87791581 29.3985088
## b      0.1551114 0.1042597 -0.01151586  0.3217386
## sigma  0.9660483 0.1181493  0.77722289  1.1548737
post_inc <- extract.samples(m_quap)
cat("P(b > 0):", round(mean(post_inc$b > 0), 3), "\n")
## P(b > 0): 0.927
cat("P(b < 0):", round(mean(post_inc$b < 0), 3), "\n")
## P(b < 0): 0.073

Total neglect accumulated across incubation showed a weak positive association with incubation duration, though the evidence was not conclusive. The posterior slope was 0.155 days per unit increase in log neglect (89% PI [−0.012, 0.322]; P(β > 0) = 0.927), with the interval just crossing zero. The intercept confirmed a mean incubation duration of 29.1 days (89% PI [28.9, 29.4]), consistent with the known biology of the species.

While most posterior weight supports a positive relationship — suggesting that nests accumulating more neglect tend toward slightly longer incubation — the uncertainty is substantial and the effect size is small. A one-unit increase in log neglect corresponds to a posterior mean increase of only 0.16 days. These results provide weak and inconclusive evidence that neglect prolongs incubation, and should be interpreted cautiously given the marginal credibility of the effect.

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = nest_gaps,
             aes(x = log_neglect_c, y = incubation_duration),
             color = "steelblue", alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(x = log_neglect_seq,
                  ymin = pred_PI[1,], ymax = pred_PI[2,]),
              fill = "black", alpha = 0.05) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(x = log_neglect_seq,
                  ymin = mu_PI[1,], ymax = mu_PI[2,]),
              fill = "black", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(aes(x = log_neglect_seq, y = mu_mean),
            linewidth = 1.2, color = "black") +
  labs(x = "log Total neglect, centered (seconds)",
       y = "Incubation duration (days)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

Incubation duration as a function of log total neglect accumulated across all three sessions. Line shows posterior mean; dark shading shows 89% PI of the mean; light shading shows 89% prediction interval.

6 Seasonal differences in neglect behaviour

To examine whether neglect behaviour varied across breeding seasons, we modelled gap frequency, probability of neglect, and neglect duration separately for each season. All three models included season-specific intercepts to allow the baseline level of neglect to vary between seasons, while a shared slope captured the common trajectory of neglect across incubation days.

6.1 Gap frequency

\[\begin{aligned} N_i &\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \\ \log(\lambda_i) &= \alpha_{season[i]} + \beta \cdot \text{dph}_c \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(1,\, 0.5) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.05) \end{aligned}\]

6.2 Probability of neglect

\[\begin{aligned} Y_i &\sim \text{Binomial}(1,\, p_i) \\ \text{logit}(p_i) &= \alpha_{season[i]} + \beta \cdot \text{dph}_c \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 1) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.2) \end{aligned}\]

6.3 Duration of neglect (given neglect occurred)

\[\begin{aligned} \log(D_i) &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\, \sigma) \\ \mu_i &= \alpha_{season[i]} + \beta \cdot \text{dph}_c \\ \alpha_j &\sim \text{Normal}(6,\, 0.5) \\ \beta &\sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 0.1) \\ \sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(1) \end{aligned}\]

Where \(\text{dph}_c\) is days prior to hatch, mean-centered. Season-specific intercepts \(\alpha_j\) capture differences in the overall level of neglect between years, while the shared slope \(\beta\) assumes a common trajectory across incubation for all seasons.

set.seed(123)
n_sim <- 50

par(mfrow = c(1, 3))

# Frequency prior
plot(NULL, xlim = c(28, 2), ylim = c(0, 15),
     xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "Expected n gaps",
     main = "Prior: Frequency")
for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(1, 1, 0.5); b <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.05)
  lines(day_seq, exp(a + b * dph_seq_c), col = col.alpha("black", 0.3))
}

# Occupancy prior
plot(NULL, xlim = c(28, 2), ylim = c(0, 1),
     xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "P(neglect)",
     main = "Prior: Probability")
for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(1, 0, 1); b <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.2)
  lines(day_seq, inv_logit(a + b * dph_seq_c), col = col.alpha("black", 0.3))
}

# Duration prior
plot(NULL, xlim = c(28, 2), ylim = c(3, 10),
     xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "log Duration (sec)",
     main = "Prior: Duration (log scale)")
for(i in 1:n_sim){
  a <- rnorm(1, 6, 0.5); b <- rnorm(1, 0, 0.1)
  lines(day_seq, a + b * dph_seq_c, col = col.alpha("black", 0.3))
}

Prior predictive simulations for seasonal models. 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
cat("=== FREQUENCY COMPARISONS ===\n")
## === FREQUENCY COMPARISONS ===
for(i in 1:(n_seasons - 1)){
  for(j in (i + 1):n_seasons){
    diff <- post_freq$a[,i] - post_freq$a[,j]
    cat(season_levels[i], "vs", season_levels[j],
        "| log diff:", round(mean(diff), 3),
        "| P(S1 > S2):", round(mean(diff > 0), 3), "\n")
  }
}
## 2019 vs 2020 | log diff: 0.222 | P(S1 > S2): 0.997 
## 2019 vs 2021 | log diff: 0.288 | P(S1 > S2): 0.999 
## 2019 vs 2023 | log diff: 0.41 | P(S1 > S2): 1 
## 2019 vs 2025 | log diff: 0.868 | P(S1 > S2): 1 
## 2020 vs 2021 | log diff: 0.065 | P(S1 > S2): 0.77 
## 2020 vs 2023 | log diff: 0.187 | P(S1 > S2): 0.97 
## 2020 vs 2025 | log diff: 0.646 | P(S1 > S2): 1 
## 2021 vs 2023 | log diff: 0.122 | P(S1 > S2): 0.863 
## 2021 vs 2025 | log diff: 0.58 | P(S1 > S2): 1 
## 2023 vs 2025 | log diff: 0.458 | P(S1 > S2): 1
cat("\n=== OCCUPANCY COMPARISONS ===\n")
## 
## === OCCUPANCY COMPARISONS ===
for(i in 1:(n_seasons - 1)){
  for(j in (i + 1):n_seasons){
    diff <- post_occ$a[,i] - post_occ$a[,j]
    cat(season_levels[i], "vs", season_levels[j],
        "| logit diff:", round(mean(diff), 3),
        "| P(S1 > S2):", round(mean(diff > 0), 3), "\n")
  }
}
## 2019 vs 2020 | logit diff: -0.256 | P(S1 > S2): 0.31 
## 2019 vs 2021 | logit diff: -0.176 | P(S1 > S2): 0.381 
## 2019 vs 2023 | logit diff: 0.155 | P(S1 > S2): 0.61 
## 2019 vs 2025 | logit diff: 0.408 | P(S1 > S2): 0.792 
## 2020 vs 2021 | logit diff: 0.08 | P(S1 > S2): 0.564 
## 2020 vs 2023 | logit diff: 0.411 | P(S1 > S2): 0.787 
## 2020 vs 2025 | logit diff: 0.664 | P(S1 > S2): 0.939 
## 2021 vs 2023 | logit diff: 0.331 | P(S1 > S2): 0.728 
## 2021 vs 2025 | logit diff: 0.584 | P(S1 > S2): 0.89 
## 2023 vs 2025 | logit diff: 0.253 | P(S1 > S2): 0.696
cat("\n=== DURATION COMPARISONS ===\n")
## 
## === DURATION COMPARISONS ===
for(i in 1:(n_seasons - 1)){
  for(j in (i + 1):n_seasons){
    diff <- post_dur$a[,i] - post_dur$a[,j]
    cat(season_levels[i], "vs", season_levels[j],
        "| log diff:", round(mean(diff), 3),
        "| P(S1 > S2):", round(mean(diff > 0), 3), "\n")
  }
}
## 2019 vs 2020 | log diff: -0.02 | P(S1 > S2): 0.472 
## 2019 vs 2021 | log diff: -0.518 | P(S1 > S2): 0.088 
## 2019 vs 2023 | log diff: -0.591 | P(S1 > S2): 0.075 
## 2019 vs 2025 | log diff: 0.607 | P(S1 > S2): 0.952 
## 2020 vs 2021 | log diff: -0.497 | P(S1 > S2): 0.073 
## 2020 vs 2023 | log diff: -0.571 | P(S1 > S2): 0.064 
## 2020 vs 2025 | log diff: 0.627 | P(S1 > S2): 0.974 
## 2021 vs 2023 | log diff: -0.074 | P(S1 > S2): 0.423 
## 2021 vs 2025 | log diff: 1.124 | P(S1 > S2): 0.999 
## 2023 vs 2025 | log diff: 1.198 | P(S1 > S2): 0.999
cat("\n=== GLOBAL SLOPES ===\n")
## 
## === GLOBAL SLOPES ===
cat("Frequency slope b:", round(mean(post_freq$b), 4),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(post_freq$b), 4),
    " P(b < 0):", round(mean(post_freq$b < 0), 3), "\n")
## Frequency slope b: 0.1651  89% PI: 0.1479 0.1822  P(b < 0): 0
cat("Occupancy slope b:", round(mean(post_occ$b), 4),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(post_occ$b), 4),
    " P(b < 0):", round(mean(post_occ$b < 0), 3), "\n")
## Occupancy slope b: 0.2119  89% PI: 0.1218 0.3005  P(b < 0): 0
cat("Duration slope b: ", round(mean(post_dur$b), 4),
    " 89% PI:", round(PI(post_dur$b), 4),
    " P(b < 0):", round(mean(post_dur$b < 0), 3), "\n")
## Duration slope b:  0.2218  89% PI: 0.1508 0.2922  P(b < 0): 0

Gap frequency differed substantially across seasons, with 2019 showing the highest baseline rate of nest exits and 2025 the lowest. The difference between 2019 and 2025 was the largest observed (log diff: 0.868; P(2019 > 2025) = 1.0), and 2019 was credibly higher than all other seasons (P > 0.997 in all comparisons). Differences among the remaining seasons were smaller and less certain, with 2020, 2021, and 2023 showing broadly similar frequency levels (P ranging from 0.77 to 0.97), though all were credibly higher than 2025 (P = 1.0 in all cases).

The probability of any neglect occurring on a given day showed weaker and less consistent seasonal differences than frequency. No pairwise comparison was fully credible, with posterior probabilities ranging from 0.31 to 0.939. The clearest signal was between 2020 and 2025 (logit diff: 0.664; P(2020 > 2025) = 0.939) and between 2021 and 2025 (logit diff: 0.584; P = 0.890), suggesting 2025 tended to have a lower probability of neglect, consistent with the frequency results. Seasons 2019, 2020, and 2021 were largely indistinguishable from each other in terms of neglect probability.

Neglect duration when it occurred showed a different pattern from frequency. The most notable contrasts involved 2025, which showed substantially shorter neglect episodes than 2021 (log diff: 1.124; P(2021 > 2025) = 0.999) and 2023 (log diff: 1.198; P(2023 > 2025) = 0.999). In contrast, 2019 and 2020 showed similar durations to each other (log diff: −0.020; P = 0.472) and were intermediate between the longer-duration seasons (2021, 2023) and the shorter-duration season (2025). Differences between 2019/2020 and 2021/2023 were suggestive but uncertain (P ranging from 0.064 to 0.088).

Across all three models, the global slope for days prior to hatch was positive and credible (frequency: β = 0.165, 89% PI [0.148, 0.182]; occupancy: β = 0.212, 89% PI [0.122, 0.301]; duration: β = 0.222, 89% PI [0.151, 0.292]; P(β < 0) = 0 in all cases), confirming that neglect — in all its dimensions — consistently declines as hatching approaches, and that this pattern is shared across all seasons.

plot(NULL, xlim = c(28, 2),
     ylim = c(0, max(E_fit_season$n_gaps) * 1.2),
     xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "Number of nest exits")
for(s in 1:n_seasons){
  idx <- E_fit_season$season == season_levels[s]
  points(jitter(E_fit_season$day_prior_hatch[idx], amount = 0.25),
         jitter(E_fit_season$n_gaps[idx], amount = 0.1),
         pch = 16, col = col.alpha(cols_season[s], 0.35), cex = 0.8)
  shade(PI_freq[[s]], day_seq, col = col.alpha(cols_season[s], 0.12))
  lines(day_seq, mu_freq[[s]], lwd = 2.5, col = cols_season[s])
}
legend("topright", legend = season_levels,
       col = cols_season[1:n_seasons], pch = 16, lwd = 2.5, bty = "n")

Expected number of nest exits per day by season across incubation. Lines show posterior mean with 89% PI. 

plot(NULL, xlim = c(28, 2), ylim = c(0, 1),
     xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "P(neglect)")
for(s in 1:n_seasons){
  shade(PI_occ[[s]], day_seq, col = col.alpha(cols_season[s], 0.12))
  lines(day_seq, mu_occ[[s]], lwd = 2.5, col = cols_season[s])
}
legend("topright", legend = season_levels,
       col = cols_season[1:n_seasons], lwd = 2.5, bty = "n")

Probability of any neglect per day by season across incubation. 

plot(NULL, xlim = c(28, 2), ylim = c(0, 10000),
     xlab = "Days prior hatch", ylab = "Duration of neglect (sec)")
for(s in 1:n_seasons){
  idx <- E_pos$season == season_levels[s]
  points(jitter(E_pos$day_prior_hatch[idx], amount = 0.25),
         E_pos$sum_gaps[idx],
         pch = 16, col = col.alpha(cols_season[s], 0.35), cex = 0.8)
  shade(PI_dur[[s]], day_seq, col = col.alpha(cols_season[s], 0.12))
  lines(day_seq, mu_dur[[s]], lwd = 2.5, col = cols_season[s])
}
legend("topright", legend = season_levels,
       col = cols_season[1:n_seasons], pch = 16, lwd = 2.5, bty = "n")

Neglect duration (seconds, log scale) given neglect occurred, by season across incubation.
